questions.question: For a diagnosis of PD-associated peritonitis to be established, at least how many of the three standard diagnostic findings (signs/symptoms of inflammation, cloudy fluid with elevated cell count, and organisms on Gram stain/culture) should be present?
questions.explanation: Diagnosis of PD peritonitis requires at least TWO of three findings, so one alone (A) is insufficient and demanding all three (C/D) is too strict. The three are: symptoms/signs of peritoneal inflammation; cloudy fluid with an elevated cell count (>100/mcL, >50% neutrophils); and organisms on Gram stain or culture. Because Gram stain is positive in fewer than half of proven cases, insisting on demonstrated bacteria would miss many episodes. Requiring two of three lets clinicians start empiric antibiotics on cloudy fluid plus pain without waiting for cultures.

questions.question: An elevated peritoneal fluid cell count supporting peritonitis is defined as greater than what value, with a predominance of neutrophils?
questions.explanation: An elevated peritoneal fluid cell count is defined as >100/mcL with a neutrophil predominance, making C correct. Lower cut-offs (10 or 50/mcL, A/B) fall within or near the normal range, where CAPD counts are usually <50 and often <10 cells/mcL; using them would over-diagnose. A threshold of 500/mcL (D) is arbitrarily high and would miss true peritonitis. This cell-count criterion is one of the three diagnostic findings, and clinically fluid turns cloudy once counts exceed roughly 50-100/mcL.

questions.question: In the diagnostic criteria for peritonitis, the elevated peritoneal fluid cell count should be due predominantly to neutrophils, meaning what proportion?
questions.explanation: The elevated cell count must be predominantly (>50%) neutrophils, so C is correct; >15% (A) is only the upper limit of the NORMAL neutrophil percentage, and >35% (B) merely raises suspicion rather than confirming. Requiring >75% (D) is too strict. Normally peritoneal white cells are mainly mononuclear with neutrophils not exceeding 15%; a value over 50% strongly suggests peritonitis. Neutrophil percentage is also raised in fungal and tuberculous peritonitis, not just common bacterial disease.

questions.question: Peritonitis is a contributing factor to approximately what percentage of deaths in patients on peritoneal dialysis?
questions.explanation: Peritonitis contributes to about 16% of deaths on PD, making B correct and remaining the Achilles' heel of the therapy. Five percent (A) understates its lethality, while 30% (C) is instead the share of PD treatment failures caused by peritonitis, and 50% (D) is far too high. These figures underscore why prevention and prompt treatment matter: peritonitis is both a leading killer and the single most common reason patients are forced off peritoneal dialysis onto hemodialysis.

questions.question: Peritonitis is the most common cause of PD treatment failure, accounting for nearly what proportion of cases?
questions.explanation: Peritonitis is the most common cause of PD treatment failure, accounting for nearly 30% of cases (C). Sixteen percent (B) is instead the proportion of PD deaths to which peritonitis contributes, so it is a distractor. Ten percent (A) understates and 45% (D) overstates the true share. This high failure rate reflects membrane damage, refractory or relapsing infection, and catheter loss, and is why disconnect (Y-set/double-bag) systems and prophylaxis have been emphasized to drive peritonitis rates down.

questions.question: Intraluminal contamination from errors in making or breaking catheter connections most typically introduces which organisms?
questions.explanation: The intraluminal route results from touch-contamination errors in making or breaking catheter/transfer-set connections, letting skin flora enter the lumen; the typical organisms are coagulase-negative staphylococci or diphtheroids (A). E. coli/Klebsiella (B) reflect the bowel source, Pseudomonas (C) the periluminal route, and Candida (D) the transvaginal route. Recognizing the pathway matters because Y-set flush-before-fill disconnect systems markedly cut these gram-positive, technique-related episodes.

questions.question: Bacteria entering the peritoneal cavity via the catheter tract from the skin surface (periluminal route) are typically which organisms?
questions.explanation: The periluminal route involves skin-surface bacteria migrating along the outside of the catheter tract into the peritoneum, and the typical organisms are S. aureus or P. aeruginosa (B). E. coli/Klebsiella (A) are bowel-source organisms, streptococci (C) are hematogenous, and diphtheroids (D) are intraluminal touch contaminants. This is why exit-site and tunnel infections with S. aureus or Pseudomonas predispose to peritonitis, and why double-cuffed catheters and exit-site antibiotic cream are protective.

questions.question: Peritonitis associated with diarrheal states or colonic instrumentation, arising from bacteria migrating across the bowel wall, typically involves which organisms?
questions.explanation: Bowel-source peritonitis arises from enteric bacteria migrating across the bowel wall, seen with diarrheal states, colonic instrumentation, or strangulated hernia; typical organisms are E. coli and Klebsiella (C). S. aureus (A) is periluminal, streptococci/staphylococci (B) are hematogenous, and Candida (D) is transvaginal. This pathway matters clinically because multiple enteric organisms, especially with anaerobes, should raise concern for an intra-abdominal abscess or perforated viscus.

questions.question: When peritonitis arises from seeding of the peritoneum from a distant site via the bloodstream, which organisms are typically involved?
questions.explanation: The hematogenous route seeds the peritoneum from a distant site through the bloodstream, and the typical organisms are streptococci and staphylococci (A). Pseudomonas (B) is periluminal, Candida (C) transvaginal, and Klebsiella (D) bowel-source. This is a less common mechanism, which is why routine blood cultures are reserved for patients who appear septic or in whom an acute surgical abdomen is suspected rather than being drawn in every peritonitis episode.

questions.question: The uncommon transvaginal ascending route of peritoneal infection may be responsible for some cases of peritonitis due to which organism?
questions.explanation: Ascending transvaginal infection travels from the vagina through the uterine tubes into the peritoneum and can account for some cases of Candida peritonitis (B). Pseudomonas (A) and E. coli (C) reflect the periluminal and bowel routes, and S. aureus (D) is periluminal. Although uncommon, this route is worth remembering in female patients, and fungal peritonitis in general is serious enough that ISPD advises prompt catheter removal once fungi are seen on Gram stain or culture.

questions.question: Standard PD solutions have an unphysiologic environment that impairs leukocyte function. What is the approximate pH of standard PD solutions?
questions.explanation: Standard PD solutions are markedly acidic, with a pH around 5 (A); values near 6.5, 7.4, or 8 (B/C/D) describe more physiologic fluids. This low pH, combined with high osmolality (1.3-1.8 times plasma) and lactate, impairs peritoneal leukocyte phagocytosis and bacterial killing by inhibiting superoxide generation. Newer normal-pH biocompatible solutions may reduce peritonitis rates, though evidence has not been consistent, underscoring that the unphysiologic dialysate itself weakens local host defense.

questions.question: The combination of high osmolality, low pH, and which anion in standard PD solutions contributes to inhibition of leukocyte superoxide production?
questions.explanation: Superoxide inhibition in peritoneal leukocytes results from high osmolality, low pH, and the lactate anion (C) present in standard solutions. Bicarbonate (A) is the buffer of newer biocompatible fluids, while acetate and citrate (B/D) are not the implicated species here. Because these conditions blunt phagocytosis and killing, host defense is compromised at baseline, which partly explains why touch contamination so readily produces infection and why biocompatible, normal-pH solutions have been investigated.

questions.question: Use of low-calcium dialysis solutions has been reported to increase the risk of peritonitis caused by which organism?
questions.explanation: Low-calcium dialysis solutions have been linked to an increased risk of Staphylococcus epidermidis peritonitis (B); Pseudomonas, E. coli, and Candida (A/C/D) are not the reported association. Peritoneal macrophage antimicrobial action is enhanced by calcium and cholecalciferol, and active vitamin D has been reported to reduce peritonitis. The 1.25-mM (2.5-mEq/L) low-calcium dialysate is popular for improving adynamic bone disease and reducing vascular calcification, but this single report has not been confirmed.

questions.question: Using appropriate culture techniques, an organism can be isolated from peritoneal fluid in approximately what proportion of clinically apparent peritonitis cases?
questions.explanation: With appropriate culture technique, an organism is isolated in over 90% of clinically apparent peritonitis episodes (C), so lower figures (50-70%, A/B) understate the yield and 'nearly 100%' (D) overstates it. This high recovery rate depends on adequate methods: sending at least 50 mL of fluid, centrifuging and inoculating blood-culture media, and incubating promptly. Failure to reach this yield, with culture-negative rates above 20%, signals that a program's laboratory technique needs review.

questions.question: Gram-positive organisms account for approximately what percentage of peritonitis episodes?
questions.explanation: Gram-positive organisms cause roughly 40-50% of peritonitis episodes (C), making them the most frequent group; coagulase-negative staphylococci alone are 12-30% and S. aureus 11-12%. Gram-negatives account for 20-30%, so lower ranges (A/B) understate gram-positive frequency and 60-70% (D) overstates it. This predominance of gram-positive, largely technique-related organisms is why disconnect systems that cut touch contamination have so effectively reduced overall peritonitis rates.

questions.question: What is the approximate frequency of fungal peritonitis among all peritonitis episodes?
questions.explanation: Fungi account for about 2-4% of peritonitis episodes (A), while Mycobacterium is roughly 1%, polymicrobial growth about 10%, and culture-negative about 15%. The higher ranges (B/C) overstate fungal frequency, and 'less than 1%' (D) understates it. Although uncommon, fungal peritonitis is serious: it usually follows recent antibiotic courses and mandates prompt catheter removal plus at least 10 days of antifungal therapy, so recognizing its low but important prevalence guides suspicion after antibacterial treatment.

questions.question: What is the most common symptom of peritonitis, present in about 95% of cases?
questions.explanation: Abdominal pain is the most common symptom, present in about 95% of peritonitis cases (B); nausea/vomiting and a feverish sensation occur in roughly 30% each and diarrhea/constipation in about 15%, so A, C, and D are less frequent. Importantly, pain can be very mild, and in the elderly the only clues may be sudden loss of residual renal function and postural hypotension. Conversely, abdominal pain in a PD patient can have non-peritonitis causes, so cloudy fluid and cell counts remain essential.

questions.question: In an elderly PD patient starting dialysis after a failed transplant in whom steroids were stopped, abdominal pain should prompt consideration of which non-peritonitis cause?
questions.explanation: In a patient who stopped steroids after a failed transplant, abdominal pain should prompt consideration of adrenal insufficiency (A) rather than assuming peritonitis. Pancreatitis, diverticulitis, and obstruction (B/C/D) are other abdominal mimics but are not the specific steroid-withdrawal association highlighted here. This matters because treating presumed peritonitis while missing adrenal crisis could be fatal, and because peritoneal fluid can mask the local tenderness of serious surgical conditions.

questions.question: Peritoneal fluid generally becomes cloudy when the cell count exceeds approximately what value?
questions.explanation: Peritoneal fluid generally turns cloudy once the cell count exceeds about 50-100/mcL (B); much lower counts (10-20, A) leave fluid clear, while 200-1000/mcL (C/D) are well above the visible threshold. Sudden cloudy fluid with abdominal symptoms usually justifies starting antibiotics, but cloudiness can also arise from fibrin, blood, chyle, malignancy, or a prolonged dwell without infection. Conversely, relatively clear fluid does not fully exclude peritonitis, so cell counts and differentials remain important.

questions.question: Cloudy peritoneal fluid has been reported with the use of which drug class, presumably by increasing peritoneal fluid triglyceride concentration?
questions.explanation: Cloudy peritoneal fluid has been reported with calcium channel blockers (C), presumably because they raise peritoneal fluid triglyceride concentration, producing a chylous appearance without infection. ACE inhibitors, beta blockers, and statins (A/B/D) are not implicated. Recognizing this non-infectious cause prevents unnecessary antibiotic courses; the differential for cloudy fluid also includes fibrin, blood, chyle, malignancy, and eosinophilia, which a peritoneal fluid differential cell count helps distinguish.

questions.question: For cell counting, a peritoneal fluid sample is typically transferred into a tube containing which anticoagulant?
questions.explanation: The peritoneal fluid sample for cell counting is placed in a tube containing EDTA (C), the standard anticoagulant that preserves cell morphology. Heparin, citrate, and fluoride (A/B/D) are used for other purposes and are not specified here. Prompt handling matters: morphologic identification of cell types becomes difficult once samples are stored more than 3-5 hours before injection into the EDTA tube. A differential count is essential to separate neutrophilic peritonitis from monocytosis or eosinophilia.

questions.question: Morphologic identification of cell types becomes difficult if effluent samples are stored beyond approximately what time before being placed in the EDTA tube?
questions.explanation: Morphologic identification of cell types becomes difficult once effluent is stored more than 3-5 hours before being placed in the EDTA tube (B); 1 hour (A) is unnecessarily strict and 12-24 hours (C/D) far exceeds the reliable window. This is why samples should be processed promptly and, for culture, delivered quickly or incubated at 37C. Accurate differential counting distinguishes true neutrophilic peritonitis from non-infectious monocytosis or eosinophilia that can also cause cloudy fluid.

questions.question: In CAPD patients, the normal absolute peritoneal fluid cell count is usually below what value?
questions.explanation: In CAPD patients the normal absolute peritoneal fluid cell count is usually below 50 cells/mcL, and often under 10 (A); the higher options (100-500/mcL, B/C/D) already fall into the abnormal, potentially peritonitic range. Note that day-dry APD patients sampled directly via the catheter with little fluid can have much higher normal counts, so context matters. Against this low baseline, a count above 100/mcL with neutrophil predominance signals peritonitis.

questions.question: In the absence of peritonitis, the percentage of neutrophils in peritoneal fluid normally does not exceed what value?
questions.explanation: Normally peritoneal white cells are mainly mononuclear, and the neutrophil percentage does not exceed 15% (B); 5% (A) is too strict, while 35% and 50% (C/D) are the suspicion and diagnostic thresholds, not the normal ceiling. A value over 50% suggests peritonitis and over 35% raises suspicion. Neutrophil percentage can also rise without peritonitis in infectious diarrhea, colitis, appendicitis, diverticulitis, pelvic inflammatory disease, menstruation, ovulation, or after a pelvic exam.

questions.question: A peritoneal fluid neutrophil percentage above what value should raise suspicion of peritonitis even though it is below the more definitive threshold?
questions.explanation: A neutrophil percentage above 35% should raise suspicion of peritonitis (C), sitting just below the more definitive >50% threshold that actively suggests it. The normal ceiling is 15% (A), so >15% or >25% (A/B) are less specific, and >45% (D) is not the stated suspicion cut-off. This graded interpretation lets clinicians act on borderline differentials, remembering that neutrophilia can also occur without peritonitis in colitis, appendicitis, pelvic inflammation, or around menstruation.

questions.question: Persistent peritoneal fluid monocytosis or lymphocytosis should prompt consideration of which diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Persistent peritoneal fluid monocytosis or lymphocytosis should prompt consideration of tuberculous peritonitis (B), not the other entities. Fungal peritonitis (A) and common bacterial disease actually raise the neutrophil percentage, and chemical or eosinophilic peritonitis (C/D) present differently. Because mycobacterial infection can appear as culture-negative peritonitis, suspecting it drives special culture of large-volume effluent sediment on solid and liquid media, and treatment with isoniazid, rifampin, ofloxacin, and pyrazinamide.

questions.question: Peritoneal fluid eosinophilia occurs most often at what point in a PD patient's course?

options.C: After several years of stable PD

questions.explanation: Peritoneal fluid eosinophilia occurs most often soon after peritoneal catheter insertion (B), usually resolving spontaneously within 2-6 weeks; it is not chiefly a feature of active bacterial peritonitis, long-stable PD, or prophylaxis (A/C/D). Suspected triggers include intraperitoneal air introduced at surgery and plasticizers leached from bags and tubing, and it is often accompanied by monocytosis. Recognizing benign eosinophilic cloudy fluid avoids unnecessary antibiotic treatment.

questions.question: Sterile peritonitis with peritoneal eosinophilia can occasionally occur in patients who have started treatment with which PD solution?
questions.explanation: Sterile peritonitis with peritoneal eosinophilia can occur in patients starting icodextrin PD solution (A); amino-acid, bicarbonate, and low-calcium solutions (B/C/D) are not the cited trigger. Eosinophilia usually coexists with monocytosis, most often appears soon after catheter insertion, and typically resolves within 2-6 weeks. Knowing icodextrin can cause a non-infectious cloudy effluent prevents misdiagnosis and needless antibiotics, though rare eosinophilia can also accompany fungal or parasitic infection.

questions.question: Culture-negative peritonitis, using proper technique, should account for no more than what proportion of episodes?
questions.explanation: Culture-negative peritonitis should not exceed 20% of episodes (C); a program above this threshold should review its culture methods. Cut-offs of 5% or 10% (A/B) are unrealistically low, and 40% (D) reflects poor technique. Good yield requires sending at least 50 mL of fluid, centrifuging and inoculating blood-culture media, prompt incubation, and sometimes hypotonic lysis to release intracellular bacteria. Persistent culture-negative cases warrant special cultures for yeast, mycobacteria, and fungi.

questions.question: To maximize the likelihood of a positive culture, what minimum volume of peritoneal fluid should be sent for culture?
questions.explanation: At least 50 mL of peritoneal fluid should be sent for culture (C), because larger volumes increase the likelihood of a positive result; 5-10 mL (A/B) reduce yield, and 200 mL (D) exceeds the stated minimum. The aliquot is centrifuged (about 3,000 g for 15 minutes) to concentrate organisms, and the pellet is inoculated into aerobic and anaerobic media. This technique helps keep culture-negative peritonitis below the 20% quality threshold and identifies the causative organism to guide therapy.

questions.question: Approximately what proportion of dialysate samples from clinical peritonitis yield positive cultures within 24-48 hours?
questions.explanation: About 70-90% of dialysate samples from clinical peritonitis yield a positive culture within 24-48 hours (C); lower ranges (A/B) understate the yield and 'nearly 100%' (D) overstates it, as fastidious organisms may need more time. This turnaround guides therapy: gram-positive isolates allow narrowing to a single agent, whereas Pseudomonas mandates two drugs. Yield is improved by adequate sample volume, centrifugation, prompt incubation, and hypotonic lysis when patients have already received antibiotics.

questions.question: Culture yield can be improved by hypotonic lysis, in which the centrifuged sediment is resuspended in what to lyse cellular elements?
questions.explanation: Culture yield is improved by hypotonic lysis, in which the centrifuged sediment is resuspended in 100 mL of sterile water (A) to lyse cellular elements and release bacteria trapped inside neutrophils. Hypertonic saline, broth alone, or povidone-iodine (B/C/D) are not the described method. This technique can boost the chance of a positive culture even after antibiotics have been started, complementing large-volume sampling and centrifugation in keeping culture-negative rates within the acceptable range.

questions.question: The Gram stain of peritoneal fluid sediment is positive in approximately what fraction of culture-proven peritonitis cases?
questions.explanation: Gram stain of peritoneal fluid sediment is positive in less than half of culture-proven peritonitis cases (A); higher fractions (two-thirds, 80%, nearly all, B/C/D) overstate its sensitivity. This is why a negative Gram stain cannot exclude peritonitis and why diagnosis rests on two of three criteria rather than demonstrated organisms. Nonetheless, Gram stain is valuable for identifying fungal peritonitis, and fluorescent acridine orange staining can improve visualization of bacteria.

questions.question: Routine blood cultures in a PD patient with peritonitis are indicated in which situation?

options.B: Only when the patient appears septic or an acute surgical abdomen is suspected
options.C: Whenever the Gram stain is negative

questions.explanation: Routine blood cultures are indicated only when the patient appears septic or an acute surgical abdomen is suspected (B), not in every peritonitis case, whenever the Gram stain is negative, or only for fungal disease (A/C/D). Most PD peritonitis is a localized intraperitoneal infection best sampled from effluent, so blood cultures add little in the stable patient. Reserving them for systemic toxicity or suspected secondary peritonitis focuses testing where bacteremia or intra-abdominal catastrophe is likely.

questions.question: Empiric antibiotic therapy for peritonitis must cover which organisms?
questions.explanation: Empiric therapy must cover BOTH gram-positive and gram-negative organisms (C), because Gram stain is often non-diagnostic and either group may be responsible. Covering only one group (A/B) leaves a major gap, and empiric fungal/mycobacterial coverage (D) is not routine. Typical regimens pair vancomycin or a first-generation cephalosporin (cefazolin) with ceftazidime or an aminoglycoside, ideally guided by center-specific sensitivity patterns, then narrowed once culture and sensitivity results return.

questions.question: The preferred route for administering antibiotics to treat PD-associated peritonitis is which of the following?
questions.explanation: Intraperitoneal administration is preferred for treating PD peritonitis (C) because it delivers high antibiotic concentrations directly to the infected cavity; oral and IV routes (A/B) and intramuscular (D) are less effective for local therapy. IV antibiotics are reserved for clinical evidence of systemic sepsis. IP dosing can be continuous (added to every bag) or intermittent (a larger dose in one dwell allowed to dwell several hours), with a loading dose given first, and doses raised 25% when residual renal function persists.

questions.question: First-generation cephalosporins such as cefazolin are often preferred over vancomycin for empiric gram-positive coverage primarily because of concern about which issue?
questions.explanation: First-generation cephalosporins such as cefazolin are often preferred to vancomycin for empiric gram-positive coverage because of the emergence of vancomycin-resistant organisms (B); vancomycin nephrotoxicity, poor penetration, or cost (A/C/D) are not the cited reasons. Vancomycin is reserved for beta-lactam-resistant organisms such as MRSA or for penicillin/cephalosporin allergy. Alternatives to vancomycin include nafcillin and clindamycin, and ciprofloxacin alone is not recommended for gram-positive infections.

questions.question: Intraperitoneal cefazolin can conveniently be given as a single daily dose of approximately what amount?
questions.explanation: IP cefazolin can be given conveniently as a single daily intermittent dose of 15 mg/kg (B); 5 mg/kg (A) underdoses, while 30-50 mg/kg (C/D) exceed the recommended amount. In patients with substantial residual renal function a roughly 25% dose increase is advised because of added drug clearance. Continuous dosing alternatively uses about 500 mg/L loading and 125 mg/L maintenance per bag. Cephalosporins lack a postantibiotic effect, so continuous dosing is generally preferred to avoid sub-MIC troughs.

questions.question: For antibiotics with renal clearance, how should dosing be adjusted in patients with substantial residual renal function (>100 mL/day urine)?
questions.explanation: For renally cleared antibiotics, patients with substantial residual renal function (>100 mL/day urine) need the dose empirically INCREASED by about 25% (B), because their added clearance would otherwise leave drug levels too low. Decreasing the dose (A/C) or leaving it unchanged (D) risks underdosing and treatment failure. This adjustment applies across the dosing tables and is a common exam point: residual function is protective for the patient but demands higher antibiotic dosing to maintain efficacy.

questions.question: In theory, aminoglycosides should be avoided if possible in which patients because of concern about nephrotoxicity?
questions.explanation: Aminoglycosides should in theory be avoided when possible in patients with residual renal function (B) because of their nephrotoxicity, which could hasten loss of that valuable function; diabetic, elderly, or anuric patients (A/C/D) are not the specific concern. In practice, short courses probably do not harm residual function, and aminoglycosides are acceptable, though otovestibular toxicity remains a worry. Intermittent single-daily dosing, avoiding constant serum levels above 2 mg/L, may reduce toxicity.

questions.question: Vancomycin is typically dosed intermittently at 15-30 mg/kg with what dosing interval for CAPD peritonitis?
questions.explanation: For intermittent CAPD dosing, vancomycin is given at 15-30 mg/kg every 5-7 days (C), reflecting its long half-life in these patients; every 12 hours or once daily (A/B) would overdose, and every 4 weeks (D) is far too infrequent. Vancomycin at 25 mg/L is stable in dialysate for 28 days at room temperature. A randomized pediatric trial showed intermittent vancomycin was as effective as continuous dosing, supporting convenient infrequent administration for gram-positive peritonitis.

questions.question: Aminoglycosides are incompatible in the same dialysis solution bag with which class of antibiotics?
questions.explanation: Aminoglycosides are incompatible with penicillins in the same dialysis solution bag (C); vancomycin, aminoglycosides, and cephalosporins (A/B/D) can be mixed together. Semisynthetic penicillins can also inactivate aminoglycosides in vitro, so their IP coadministration should be avoided, which is relevant when treating Pseudomonas with an aminoglycoside plus an anti-Pseudomonas penicillin. Knowing these compatibilities prevents inadvertent drug inactivation and preserves the potency of combination regimens.

questions.question: Vancomycin at 25 mg/L is stable in dialysis solution stored at room temperature for approximately how long?
questions.explanation: Vancomycin at 25 mg/L is stable in dialysis solution stored at room temperature for about 28 days (D); 24 hours, 7 days, or 14 days (A/B/C) understate its stability, though high ambient temperatures shorten it. By comparison, gentamicin (8 mg/L) is stable 14 days and cefazolin (500 mg/L) about 8 days at room temperature. This long stability supports premixing and infrequent intermittent vancomycin dosing, making outpatient management of gram-positive peritonitis more practical.

questions.question: Heparin is commonly added to dialysis solution during peritonitis at what concentration to reduce the risk of catheter obstruction from fibrin clots?
questions.explanation: Heparin is added to dialysis solution at 500-1,000 units/L during peritonitis (B) to prevent fibrin clots from obstructing the catheter; far lower or higher concentrations (A/C/D) are not standard. Peritonitis commonly causes fibrinous clots, and heparin is continued until the infection resolves and clots are no longer visible in the effluent. Cefazolin stability is unaffected by heparin, whereas gentamicin stability is reduced by it, a practical consideration when co-administering in the same bag.

questions.question: Oral prophylaxis with which agent, given during antibiotic therapy, may prevent some cases of Candida peritonitis in high-risk programs?
questions.explanation: Oral nystatin (B) given during antibiotic therapy is the antifungal prophylaxis studied to prevent Candida peritonitis, since most fungal episodes follow antibiotic courses. Fluconazole, amphotericin, and ketoconazole (A/C/D) are treatments, not the prophylactic agent described. Evidence is mixed, so nystatin prophylaxis is recommended mainly in programs with high baseline fungal peritonitis rates. This reflects the principle that antibiotic-driven fungal overgrowth is a key predisposing factor for Candida peritonitis.

questions.question: In a PD patient with suspected underlying intra-abdominal pathology, free intraperitoneal air on an upright chest radiograph may suggest what, provided no recent laparotomy or transfer-set change occurred?
questions.explanation: Free intraperitoneal air on an upright chest radiograph may suggest a perforated viscus (B), provided no recent laparotomy or transfer-set change has occurred; it is not a marker of pancreatitis, tuberculous, or fungal peritonitis (A/C/D). Free air is otherwise unusual in CAPD patients, though it occurs more commonly with cyclers. Because peritoneal fluid can mask local tenderness of surgical conditions, a chest radiograph is obtained whenever underlying intra-abdominal pathology (secondary peritonitis) is suspected.

questions.question: In PD patients using icodextrin-based solutions, which enzyme is superior for the diagnosis of acute pancreatitis?
questions.explanation: In patients using icodextrin-based solutions, lipase (B) is superior to amylase for diagnosing acute pancreatitis. Amylase (A) is unreliable here, and trypsin or alkaline phosphatase (C/D) are not the recommended markers. In general, serum amylase over three times normal suggests pancreatitis, but severe pancreatitis can occur with only slight elevations, and effluent amylase is insensitive. An effluent amylase above 100 units/dL suggests pancreatitis or another intra-abdominal catastrophe, warranting further evaluation.

questions.question: An effluent (peritoneal fluid) amylase level above what value is suggestive of pancreatitis or another intra-abdominal catastrophe?
questions.explanation: An effluent (peritoneal fluid) amylase above 100 units/dL suggests pancreatitis or another intra-abdominal catastrophe (C); lower thresholds (20-50 units/dL, A/B) are non-specific and 300 units/dL (D) is not the stated cut-off. Effluent amylase is otherwise insensitive because it may be only slightly elevated even in severe pancreatitis. Serum amylase over three times normal is more suggestive, and in icodextrin users lipase outperforms amylase, together helping detect secondary intra-abdominal disease.

questions.question: During peritonitis, increased peritoneal permeability leads to rapid glucose absorption, which can result in which consequence?
questions.explanation: During peritonitis the peritoneum becomes more permeable, so glucose is absorbed rapidly, reducing ultrafiltration and risking fluid overload (B); it does not increase ultrafiltration or reduce protein loss (A/D), and hyperglycemia rather than hypoglycemia (C) results. Higher dialysate glucose concentrations and shorter dwell times may be needed to maintain ultrafiltration, and insulin should be titrated in diabetics. Protein losses are also temporarily increased, all reflecting the transiently leakier inflamed membrane.

questions.question: During peritonitis with constipation, which class of medication should be temporarily discontinued because it can worsen constipation?
questions.explanation: When constipation accompanies peritonitis, calcium-containing phosphate binders (A) should be temporarily stopped because they can cause or worsen constipation; beta blockers, statins, and PPIs (B/C/D) are not the cited culprits. Constipation impairs drainage of instilled dialysate and may itself predispose to enteric peritonitis. This links to prevention: treating constipation and enteritis, correcting hypokalemia, and possibly using lactulose may lower the risk of peritonitis from enteric organisms.

questions.question: After bacterial contamination of the peritoneal cavity, the incubation period for most organisms is approximately what interval?
questions.explanation: After bacterial contamination of the peritoneal cavity, the incubation period for most organisms is about 12-48 hours (B); hours (A) is too short and days to weeks (C/D) too long. This window explains why, after a recognized break in sterile technique, prompt prophylactic antibiotics plus a transfer-set change are advised to try to prevent peritonitis, even though efficacy is not proven. A short oral course such as ciprofloxacin may be added while flushing with an antistaphylococcal antibiotic.

questions.question: With effective treatment, a patient with peritonitis should generally begin to improve clinically within what time frame?
questions.explanation: With effective treatment, patients should begin improving clinically within 12-48 hours (B), with a falling total cell count and neutrophil percentage; expecting improvement within hours (A) is unrealistic and waiting days to a week (C/D) delays needed reassessment. If there is no improvement by 48 hours, repeat cell count and culture are required. Refractory peritonitis, defined as failure to clear after 5 days of appropriate antibiotics, then warrants catheter removal to limit morbidity and membrane damage.

questions.question: For coagulase-negative staphylococcal peritonitis with prompt improvement, the total recommended duration of antimicrobial therapy is how long?
questions.explanation: Coagulase-negative staphylococcal (and Enterococcus) peritonitis with prompt improvement should be treated for a total of 14 days (B); 7 days (A) is too short, while 21 days (C) is reserved for S. aureus and gram-negative disease and 28 days (D) is excessive. Many CoNS organisms reported resistant to first-generation cephalosporins are still killed by the high levels achieved intraperitoneally, so if the patient is responding there is usually no need to change the antibiotic.

questions.question: Staphylococcus aureus peritonitis requires antimicrobial therapy for what total duration?
questions.explanation: S. aureus peritonitis requires 3 weeks of antimicrobial therapy (C); 1-2 weeks (A/B) are insufficient for this more virulent organism, and 6 weeks (D) is longer than recommended. Rifampin may be added as an adjunct to prevent relapse or repeat S. aureus peritonitis, remembering it induces CYP3A4. Crucially, S. aureus peritonitis with a concurrent exit-site or tunnel infection is unlikely to respond to antibiotics without catheter removal, and nasal carriage eradication helps prevent recurrence.

questions.question: Rifampin, considered an adjunct to prevent relapse of S. aureus peritonitis, induces which hepatic enzyme, an important drug-interaction consideration?
questions.explanation: Rifampin induces cytochrome P450 CYP3A4 (A), an important interaction to remember when patients take other drugs metabolized by this pathway; it does not act via alcohol dehydrogenase, xanthine oxidase, or monoamine oxidase (B/C/D). Rifampin is used both as an adjunct to prevent relapse of S. aureus peritonitis and to eradicate nasal carriage (300 mg bid for 5 days every 3 months). Rising resistance to rifampin and mupirocin means its use should be judicious and cultures rechecked.

questions.question: For Enterococcus peritonitis, the generally employed regimen is ampicillin or vancomycin plus which additional agent?
questions.explanation: For Enterococcus peritonitis, ampicillin or vancomycin is combined with an aminoglycoside (B); a cephalosporin, metronidazole, or a quinolone (A/C/D) are not the standard partners, as enterococci are intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins. If sensitivity testing shows vancomycin resistance, linezolid or quinupristin/dalfopristin is needed. Like coagulase-negative staphylococcal disease, uncomplicated Enterococcus peritonitis that responds promptly is treated for a total of 14 days.

questions.question: Eradication of nasal S. aureus carriage may help prevent recurrent peritonitis. One recommended regimen is intranasal mupirocin used how often?

options.A: Twice daily for 5 days every 4 weeks

questions.explanation: Nasal S. aureus carriage can be eradicated with intranasal mupirocin twice daily for 5 days every 4 weeks (A), or alternatively oral rifampin 300 mg bid for 5 days every 3 months; continuous or one-off short regimens (B/C/D) are not the described schedule. Patients who develop S. aureus peritonitis are usually nasal carriers, so eradication helps prevent recurrence, and success should be documented by repeat cultures. Resistance to both mupirocin and rifampin is increasingly common.

questions.question: When a Pseudomonas species is recovered from peritoneal fluid, how many antibiotics are considered mandatory for treatment?
questions.explanation: When Pseudomonas is recovered, TWO antibiotics are mandatory (B); a single agent (A) risks failure, and three or four (C/D) are not required for uncomplicated Pseudomonas peritonitis. Typically an IP aminoglycoside is continued with an added IP third-generation cephalosporin or an IV anti-Pseudomonas penicillin such as piperacillin (avoiding IP coadministration, which inactivates aminoglycosides). Pseudomonas is hard to eradicate, relapse risk is high, and catheter removal is needed in up to two-thirds of cases.

questions.question: Pseudomonas peritonitis requires catheter removal in up to approximately what proportion of cases?
questions.explanation: Pseudomonas peritonitis requires catheter removal in up to two-thirds of cases (D); one-tenth, one-third, or one-half (A/B/C) understate this high rate. Pseudomonas is difficult to eradicate and carries a high relapse risk, which is why two antibiotics are mandatory and prolonged, and why relapsing gram-negative peritonitis, especially Pseudomonas, should prompt strong consideration of catheter removal. After removal, antibiotics are continued for another 2 weeks, particularly with Pseudomonas.

questions.question: Because semisynthetic penicillins can inactivate aminoglycosides in vitro, what should be avoided when both are used?
questions.explanation: Because semisynthetic penicillins can inactivate aminoglycosides in vitro, IP coadministration of the two should be avoided (B); the issue is not oral use, once-daily dosing, or any concurrent use whatsoever (A/C/D), since the penicillin can be given by a different route such as IV. This is directly relevant to Pseudomonas therapy, where an IP aminoglycoside is paired with an anti-Pseudomonas penicillin like piperacillin given IV. Aminoglycosides and penicillins are also incompatible mixed in the same bag.

questions.question: For uncomplicated gram-negative peritonitis, the duration of therapy is at least 2 and preferably how many weeks?
questions.explanation: For uncomplicated gram-negative peritonitis, therapy lasts at least 2 and preferably 3 weeks (B); 1 week (A) is too short, and 5-6 weeks (C/D) exceed the recommendation. A single non-Pseudomonas isolate can often be treated with continuation of the initial IP third-generation cephalosporin or aminoglycoside alone. Gram-negative recovery carries important implications: difficult eradication and relapse (especially Pseudomonas), possible unsuspected intra-abdominal pathology, and aminoglycoside otovestibular toxicity.

questions.question: If the peritoneal catheter is removed for gram-negative peritonitis, appropriate antibiotics should be continued (PO or IV) for approximately how long afterward, especially with Pseudomonas?
questions.explanation: If the catheter is removed for gram-negative peritonitis, antibiotics should be continued (PO or IV) for another 2 weeks (C), especially with Pseudomonas; 3 days or 1 week (A/B) is too brief and 6 weeks (D) longer than advised. This post-removal course clears residual infection while the patient is temporarily on hemodialysis. Gram-negative, particularly Pseudomonas, peritonitis is prone to relapse and often demands catheter removal, so completing adequate post-removal therapy is important to prevent recurrence.

questions.question: The major risk factor for Stenotrophomonas (formerly Xanthomonas) maltophilia peritonitis is which of the following?
questions.explanation: The major risk factor for Stenotrophomonas (formerly Xanthomonas) maltophilia peritonitis is previous use of broad-spectrum antibiotics (B); diabetes, low-calcium dialysate, or nasal S. aureus carriage (A/C/D) are not the cited factor. These organisms are usually highly resistant, so medical therapy requires two antibiotics, often including cotrimoxazole, extended to a minimum of 3-4 weeks, and catheter removal is usually required. This illustrates how prior antibiotic exposure selects for difficult, resistant pathogens.

questions.question: Peritonitis due to multiple gram-positive organisms generally responds to antibiotics, with about what proportion resolving without catheter removal?
questions.explanation: Peritonitis due to multiple gram-positive organisms generally responds to antibiotics, with about 60% resolving without catheter removal (B); 30% (A) understates and 80% or nearly all (C/D) overstate the success rate. In sharp contrast, multiple enteric organisms, especially with anaerobes, suggest an intra-abdominal abscess or perforated viscus, carry increased mortality, and need triple antibiotics plus surgical evaluation. Distinguishing gram-positive from enteric polymicrobial peritonitis guides both prognosis and management.

questions.question: When multiple enteric organisms, particularly with anaerobes, are grown, which possibility must be strongly considered?
questions.explanation: When multiple enteric organisms grow, particularly with anaerobes, an intra-abdominal abscess or perforated viscus must be strongly considered (B); fungal superinfection, tuberculous peritonitis, or contamination (A/C/D) are not the primary concern. Perforated diverticulum, tubo-ovarian abscess, cholecystitis, appendicitis, perforated ulcer, and pancreatitis enter the differential, and mortality is increased. Management uses triple antibiotics covering gram-positive, gram-negative, and anaerobic organisms plus surgical evaluation.

questions.question: Initial triple-antibiotic therapy for enteric polymicrobial peritonitis typically includes an IP aminoglycoside, IP vancomycin, and which agent for anaerobic coverage?
questions.explanation: Triple therapy for enteric polymicrobial peritonitis adds metronidazole (A) for anaerobic coverage, alongside an IP aminoglycoside and IP vancomycin; ceftazidime, fluconazole, or ciprofloxacin (B/C/D) do not provide the needed anaerobic activity here. This regimen targets gram-positive, gram-negative, and anaerobic organisms simultaneously because multiple enteric isolates raise concern for an abscess or perforated viscus. A surgical evaluation should be obtained and management individualized given the increased mortality.

questions.question: For culture-negative peritonitis in an improving patient, many authorities recommend discontinuing the gram-negative coverage after approximately how many days?
questions.explanation: For culture-negative peritonitis in an improving patient, many authorities discontinue the gram-negative coverage (ceftazidime or aminoglycoside) after 3 days (B); 1 day (A) is premature and 7-14 days (C/D) prolongs unnecessary exposure, though the formal ISPD recommendation is 14 days of both agents. If cultures stay negative but the patient does not improve, special cultures for yeast, mycobacteria, and fungi are indicated, and growth can appear only after 5-7 days, so incubation should be prolonged.

questions.question: Mycobacterial peritonitis sometimes presents as culture-negative peritonitis. Which agents are generally NOT recommended in dialysis patients as part of the treatment regimen?
questions.explanation: For mycobacterial peritonitis, streptomycin and ethambutol are generally NOT recommended in dialysis patients (C); the standard multidrug regimen instead uses isoniazid, rifampin, ofloxacin, and pyrazinamide (making A/B/D part of appropriate therapy). Mycobacterial infection can present as culture-negative peritonitis, so suspicion prompts special large-volume effluent culture on solid (Lowenstein-Jensen) and liquid media. Catheter removal is often but not always required, provided prompt therapy is given.

questions.question: According to ISPD recommendations, what is the appropriate management once fungi are identified by Gram stain or culture?

options.A: Continue PD with IP antifungals only
options.B: Prompt removal of the catheter plus antifungal therapy
options.C: Add oral nystatin and continue PD

questions.explanation: ISPD recommends prompt catheter removal as soon as fungi are identified by Gram stain or culture (B), together with antifungal therapy for at least 10 days and maintenance on hemodialysis; continuing PD with IP antifungals, adding nystatin, or increasing the aminoglycoside (A/C/D) are inappropriate. Fungal peritonitis is serious, so delaying removal risks membrane damage and death. A new catheter may be placed 4-6 weeks later once at least 1 week has passed since all clinical evidence of peritonitis subsided.

questions.question: Which condition is a recognized predisposing factor for fungal peritonitis?

options.B: Recent antibiotic treatment for bacterial peritonitis

questions.explanation: Recent antibiotic treatment for bacterial peritonitis is a key predisposing factor for fungal peritonitis (B), because antibiotics permit fungal overgrowth; hypertension, hyperkalemia, and high albumin (A/C/D) are not risk factors. Other predisposers include diabetes mellitus, immunosuppression (immunosuppressive therapy, HIV), and malnutrition with low serum albumin. This is why nystatin prophylaxis during antibiotic courses is considered in high-risk programs, and why fungi should be suspected after antibacterial treatment.

questions.question: After catheter removal for fungal peritonitis, a new catheter can sometimes be inserted at 4-6 weeks provided how much time has elapsed since all clinical evidence of peritonitis has subsided?
questions.explanation: After catheter removal for fungal peritonitis, a new catheter may be inserted 4-6 weeks later provided at least 1 week has elapsed since all clinical evidence of peritonitis subsided (B); 24 hours (A) is far too soon, and 2-4 weeks (C/D) are not the stated interval. Candida is the most common species, but many fungi can be responsible. Prolonged oral antifungals (such as fluconazole or flucytosine) may be added to limit adhesion formation, and the patient is maintained on hemodialysis meanwhile.

questions.question: Voriconazole or posaconazole is an alternative to amphotericin B when filamentous fungi are cultured, but these agents cannot be used alone for which infection?
questions.explanation: Voriconazole or posaconazole is an alternative to amphotericin B for filamentous fungi, but neither can be used alone for Candida peritonitis (A), even with catheter removal; they are not restricted specifically for Aspergillus, mycobacterial, or Pseudomonas disease (B/C/D). Recommended dosages match normal renal function except flucytosine, whose dose must be reduced. Because Candida is the most prevalent fungal species, this caveat is clinically important: adequate anti-Candida therapy requires appropriate agents plus catheter removal.

questions.question: Refractory peritonitis is defined as failure of peritonitis to clear after how many days of appropriate antibiotics?
questions.explanation: Refractory peritonitis is defined as failure to clear after 5 days of appropriate antibiotics (C); 2-3 days (A/B) is too soon to declare failure and 10 days (D) delays needed action. At that point catheter removal is indicated to reduce morbidity and preserve the peritoneal membrane, rather than prolonging antibiotic exposure with its risks of superinfection and membrane damage. If an intra-abdominal abscess is suspected, imaging (ultrasound, CT, or gallium scan) guides possible surgical drainage.

questions.question: After catheter removal for peritonitis, a conservative approach recommends waiting how long before inserting a new catheter?
questions.explanation: After catheter removal for peritonitis, a conservative approach waits 4-6 weeks before inserting a new catheter (C); 1 week or 2-3 weeks (A/B) may be too soon depending on severity, and 3 months (D) is longer than needed. The safe interval is controversial and depends on peritonitis severity and whether fungal or tunnel infection was present. Resumption of PD is possible in roughly half of patients, though the dialysis prescription may need changing to achieve adequate clearance and ultrafiltration.

questions.question: Relapsing peritonitis is defined as peritonitis with the same organism occurring within what interval of stopping antimicrobial therapy?
questions.explanation: Relapsing peritonitis is defined as peritonitis with the SAME organism within 4 weeks of stopping antimicrobial therapy (C); 1-2 weeks (A/B) is too short a window and 8 weeks (D) too long. S. epidermidis, gram-negatives, or culture-negative organisms are commonly involved. Relapsing gram-negative disease, especially Pseudomonas, should prompt strong consideration of catheter removal; for less serious relapses a new catheter can be inserted simultaneously with removal, placed well away from the old exit site.

questions.question: For relapsing gram-negative peritonitis, catheter removal should be strongly considered, especially in cases of infection with which organism?
questions.explanation: In relapsing gram-negative peritonitis, catheter removal with or without surgical exploration should be strongly considered, especially with Pseudomonas (B); E. coli, Klebsiella, or Enterococcus (A/C/D) are not singled out here. Pseudomonas is hard to eradicate, and if medical treatment is chosen the aminoglycoside should be given intermittently or an alternative agent used. Relapse means the same organism returns within 4 weeks of stopping therapy, reflecting persistent infection along the catheter or biofilm.

questions.question: In managing less serious relapsing peritonitis, a useful approach obviating hemodialysis is simultaneous catheter removal and reinsertion, with the new catheter placed where?

options.B: Well away from the old exit site
options.C: On the opposite side of the abdomen only

questions.explanation: In managing less serious relapsing peritonitis, the new catheter is inserted well away from the old exit site (B), allowing simultaneous removal and replacement that avoids a hemodialysis interval; reusing the same site or tunnel (A/D) or restricting to the opposite side only (C) is not the described approach. This single-procedure exchange has been particularly useful for relapsing coagulase-negative staphylococcal peritonitis, where infection is often catheter-associated but the peritoneum is otherwise treatable.

questions.question: Recent studies indicate that compared with relapsing peritonitis, recurrent peritonitis episodes have which prognostic feature?
questions.explanation: Recurrent peritonitis episodes have a WORSE prognosis than relapsing ones (B), and the two are probably distinct clinical entities caused by different bacterial spectra; they are not better or identical (A/C), nor free of further risk (D). Repeat peritonitis generally responds to antibiotics but carries substantial risk of further relapsing or repeat episodes. Distinguishing relapsing (same organism within 4 weeks), recurrent, and repeat peritonitis matters because it informs prognosis and the aggressiveness of management.

questions.question: Controlled studies of fibrinolytic enzymes such as streptokinase and urokinase for refractory or relapsing peritonitis have shown what result compared with catheter removal and replacement?

options.B: They have not been proven effective
options.C: They are equally effective and cheaper

questions.explanation: Controlled studies have not proven fibrinolytic enzymes such as streptokinase and urokinase effective for refractory or relapsing peritonitis compared with catheter removal and replacement (B); they are not superior, cheaper-but-equal, or curative for Pseudomonas (A/C/D). The rationale was to release bacteria entrapped in fibrin along the catheter, but evidence does not support it. This reinforces that catheter removal, not fibrinolysis, is the reliable strategy when infection persists despite appropriate antibiotics.

questions.question: Which of the following is an accepted indication for short-term prophylactic systemic antibiotics in PD patients?

options.A: Routine daily use to prevent peritonitis
options.C: For all patients with erythema at the exit site

questions.explanation: Short-term prophylactic systemic antibiotics are beneficial before peritoneal catheter placement (B), using vancomycin or cefazolin; routine daily use, treating all exit-site erythema, or continuous use in carriers (A/C/D) are not accepted indications, since prophylactic antibiotics do not otherwise prevent peritonitis. Other valid settings are before invasive procedures (dental, colonoscopy, hysteroscopy, cholecystectomy) and after accidental contamination. This targeted, situational prophylaxis contrasts with ineffective ongoing use.

questions.question: For prevention of bacteremia during dental procedures in PD patients, which prophylactic antibiotic is recommended?
questions.explanation: Amoxicillin 2 g (A) is recommended to prevent bacteremia during dental procedures in PD patients; vancomycin, ampicillin plus aminoglycoside, or ceftazidime (B/C/D) are not the dental regimen. Ampicillin plus an aminoglycoside is instead used before colonoscopy, colonoscopic polypectomy, hysteroscopy, or cholecystectomy, and vancomycin or cefazolin before catheter placement. These procedure-specific prophylaxis regimens target expected flora, unlike routine antibiotics, which do not prevent peritonitis.

questions.question: Compared with single-cuffed catheters, double-cuffed catheters are described as being what with regard to peritonitis risk?

options.A: Clearly superior in decreasing peritonitis risk

questions.explanation: Double-cuffed catheters are clearly superior to single-cuffed catheters in decreasing peritonitis risk (A); they are not merely equivalent, inferior, or useful only for exit-site infection (B/C/D). The second cuff provides an added barrier to periluminal movement of S. aureus into the peritoneum. No particular catheter design has been definitively shown better than the standard silicone double-cuff catheter for preventing peritonitis, though whether double cuffs also lower exit-site infection rates is more controversial.

questions.question: As a perioperative measure to reduce peritonitis, the subcutaneous cuff should be positioned approximately what distance from the exit site?
questions.explanation: The subcutaneous cuff should be positioned 2 to 3 cm from the exit site (B); 0.5-1 cm (A) is too close and 5-10 cm (C/D) too far. This is one of several perioperative measures to reduce peritonitis: the exit site should be directed downward or laterally, the tunnel incision should not exceed the catheter diameter, the exit hole should be the smallest possible, and no sutures should anchor the catheter at the exit site. Proper cuff placement supports tissue ingrowth and barrier function.

questions.question: Which electrolyte disturbance is associated with an increased risk of enteric peritonitis and should be treated if present?
questions.explanation: Hypokalemia is associated with an increased risk of enteric peritonitis and should be treated if present (B); hypercalcemia, hypernatremia, and hyperphosphatemia (A/C/D) are not the cited association. Low potassium may impair bowel motility and promote bacterial translocation. This fits with other enteric-risk factors: severe constipation and enteritis also predispose to peritonitis from enteric organisms, so treating constipation, correcting hypokalemia, and possibly using lactulose are preventive measures.

questions.question: An observational study suggested that treatment with which agent may reduce the risk of peritonitis?
questions.explanation: An observational study suggested that lactulose treatment may reduce the risk of peritonitis (A); metformin, aspirin, and vitamin C (B/C/D) are not implicated. The proposed benefit relates to reducing constipation and enteric bacterial translocation, consistent with the observation that constipation and enteritis predispose to enteric peritonitis. Alongside correcting hypokalemia and treating bacterial enteritis, this is part of a bowel-focused prevention strategy, though the evidence is observational rather than from a controlled trial.

questions.question: Which finding at the catheter exit site generally indicates the presence of infection?
questions.explanation: Purulent drainage from the exit site generally indicates infection (B); erythema alone (A) may or may not represent infection, and serous crusting or mild tenderness (C/D) are non-specific. This distinction guides therapy: erythema alone is treated topically with hypertonic saline, hydrogen peroxide, or mupirocin, whereas purulent drainage warrants Gram stain and culture-directed systemic antibiotics. Exit-site infections predispose to peritonitis, and about one-fifth of peritonitis episodes are temporally associated with them.

questions.question: Approximately what proportion of peritonitis episodes are temporally associated with exit-site and tunnel infections?
questions.explanation: Approximately one-fifth of peritonitis episodes are temporally associated with exit-site and tunnel infections (B); one-tenth, one-third, or one-half (A/C/D) misstate this proportion. This close link is why exit-site infection is treated aggressively and why prophylactic exit-site antibiotic cream reduces peritonitis. Exit-site infections are predominantly caused by S. aureus and P. aeruginosa, and S. aureus peritonitis with a concurrent exit-site or tunnel infection often cannot be cured without catheter removal.

questions.question: The incidence of exit-site infections is approximately 1 episode per what interval?
questions.explanation: The incidence of exit-site infections is about 1 episode every 24-48 patient-months (B); more frequent (6-12 or 3-6 patient-months, A/D) or much rarer (60-100 patient-months, C) figures are incorrect. Patients with previous infections tend to have a higher frequency. Because roughly one-fifth of peritonitis episodes are linked to exit-site or tunnel infection, reducing exit-site infection through daily antibiotic cream and good exit-site care is a central strategy for preventing peritonitis overall.

questions.question: Exit-site infections are predominantly caused by which organisms?
questions.explanation: Exit-site infections are predominantly due to S. aureus and P. aeruginosa (A); E. coli/Klebsiella, coagulase-negative staphylococci alone, or Candida/Aspergillus (B/C/D) are not the main causes, with S. epidermidis responsible in fewer than 20% of patients. About 45% of patients are nasal S. aureus carriers, and carriage is associated with both exit-site infection and peritonitis, so eradicating the carrier state aids management. These same organisms dominate the periluminal route into the peritoneum.

questions.question: Approximately what percentage of PD patients are nasal carriers of S. aureus, a state associated with exit-site infection and peritonitis?
questions.explanation: About 45% of PD patients are nasal carriers of S. aureus (C); 15% or 25% (A/B) understate and 70% (D) overstates this rate. Nasal carriage is associated with exit-site infection and peritonitis, so its eradication is helpful in management, using intranasal mupirocin or oral rifampin regimens. Patients who develop S. aureus peritonitis are usually carriers, which underlies the strategy of screening and decolonizing carriers to prevent recurrent staphylococcal catheter and peritoneal infections.

questions.question: For an exit site with erythema alone, which is an appropriate topical treatment?
questions.explanation: For an exit site with erythema alone, mupirocin 2% ointment (B) is an appropriate topical treatment, along with hypertonic saline or hydrogen peroxide; an oral quinolone, IP vancomycin, or surgical deroofing (A/C/D) are reserved for more advanced infection. Erythema alone may or may not represent true infection, so escalation to systemic, culture-directed antibiotics is reserved for purulent drainage. Note mupirocin ointment must not be used on polyurethane catheters, as its polyethylene glycol degrades them.

questions.question: Mupirocin ointment should not be used with polyurethane catheters because of which problem?

options.B: Polyethylene glycol in the ointment degrades the polyurethane

questions.explanation: Mupirocin ointment should not be used with polyurethane catheters because the polyethylene glycol in the ointment degrades the polyurethane and can destroy the catheter (B); it does not cause fungal overgrowth, become inactivated, or increase gram-negative infection (A/C/D). Affected catheters include several made by Vas-Cath and the Cruz/Corpak catheter. Ciprofloxacin otologic solution can be used with polyurethane catheters instead, though its efficacy for exit-site infection is unknown.

questions.question: For an exit-site infection with gram-positive organisms on culture, appropriate first-line therapy is which of the following?

options.A: An oral cephalosporin or antistaphylococcal penicillin

questions.explanation: For an exit-site infection with gram-positive organisms on culture, first-line therapy is an oral cephalosporin or antistaphylococcal penicillin (A); IP ceftazidime or IV vancomycin (B/D) are not first-line for localized exit-site infection, and metronidazole (C) targets anaerobes. Therapy should be guided by Gram stain and culture. If there is no improvement after 1 week, oral rifampin 600 mg/day may be added, and unresolved infection at 2 weeks may require surgical deroofing, cuff shaving, or catheter removal.

questions.question: If an exit-site infection has not improved after 1 week of appropriate treatment based on culture and sensitivity, which oral agent may be added?
questions.explanation: If an exit-site infection has not improved after 1 week of appropriate culture-directed treatment, oral rifampin 600 mg per day (A) may be added; fluconazole, metronidazole, or nystatin (B/C/D) do not target the usual gram-positive exit-site pathogens. Rifampin adds antistaphylococcal potency but induces CYP3A4, a drug-interaction caution. If infection persists to 2 weeks, a surgical approach (deroofing, outer-cuff shaving, or catheter removal) is considered, especially with tunnel involvement or Pseudomonas.

questions.question: If an exit-site infection has not resolved after approximately how long, a surgical approach such as deroofing, cuff shaving, or catheter removal may be required?
questions.explanation: If an exit-site infection has not resolved after about 2 weeks, a surgical approach such as deroofing, outer-cuff shaving, or catheter removal may be required (C); 3 days or 1 week (A/B) is too soon to declare medical failure, and 6 weeks (D) delays needed intervention. Before surgery, oral rifampin 600 mg/day can be added at 1 week if there is no improvement. Tunnel infection, better seen on ultrasound, often warrants early cuff excision, and P. aeruginosa exit-site infection warrants early removal.

questions.question: For exit-site infections, the minimum length of antibiotic treatment is how long, continued until the site appears entirely normal?
questions.explanation: Two weeks is the minimum treatment length for exit-site infections, continued until the exit site appears entirely normal (C); 3 days or 1 week (A/B) is inadequate and 4 weeks (D) exceeds the stated minimum. P. aeruginosa exit-site infection probably needs at least 3 weeks and early consideration of catheter removal. Because incompletely treated exit-site infection can progress to tunnel infection and peritonitis, therapy is guided by culture and continued until clinical resolution rather than a fixed short course.

questions.question: Exit-site infections caused by P. aeruginosa probably require treatment for at least how long?
questions.explanation: Exit-site infections caused by P. aeruginosa probably require at least 3 weeks of treatment (C); 1-2 weeks (A/B) is the general minimum for other organisms but insufficient for Pseudomonas, and 6 weeks (D) is not specified. Early catheter removal should be considered for pseudomonal exit-site infection or when tunnel infection is present, with single-procedure replacement at a different site often feasible. More serious pseudomonal infections may need IP ceftazidime or an aminoglycoside in addition to oral therapy.

questions.question: When treating gram-negative exit-site infection with oral quinolones, ingestion of multivalent cations such as calcium, iron, and zinc should be avoided within what time of the drug?
questions.explanation: When treating gram-negative exit-site infection with oral quinolones, multivalent cations (calcium, iron, zinc, antacids) must not be ingested within 2 hours of the drug (C); 30 minutes or 1 hour (A/B) is too short a gap and 6 hours (D) is unnecessarily long. These cations chelate the quinolone and impair its gastrointestinal absorption, undermining efficacy. Oral quinolones are attractive because they achieve effective levels, but this interaction, also relevant for phosphate binders, must be managed for adequate dosing.

questions.question: Because of its efficacy against both gram-positive and gram-negative infections, which daily exit-site cream has been advocated as the prophylaxis of choice for PD patients?
questions.explanation: Daily gentamicin cream at the exit site has been advocated as the prophylaxis of choice (B) because it is effective against both gram-positive and gram-negative infections; mupirocin cream (A) works well against S. aureus but not gram-negatives, and bacitracin or silver sulfadiazine (C/D) are not the advocated agents. In a trial, gentamicin cream matched mupirocin for S. aureus, reduced P. aeruginosa and other gram-negative catheter infections, and cut peritonitis, particularly gram-negative, by about 35%.

questions.question: In children, use of which antiseptic solution instead of povidone-iodine was associated with a significant decrease in exit-site infections?
questions.explanation: Use of chlorhexidine (A) instead of povidone-iodine was associated with a significant decrease in exit-site infections in children; hydrogen peroxide, alcohol, or normal saline (B/C/D) were not the compared antiseptics. Polyhexanide solution also appears superior to povidone-iodine. Alongside daily antibiotic exit-site cream, leaving the catheter embedded subcutaneously before exteriorizing, and good exit-site technique, antiseptic choice is part of a broader strategy to prevent catheter infections and thereby peritonitis.

questions.question: S. aureus peritonitis with a concurrent exit-site or tunnel infection is unlikely to respond to antibiotic therapy without which intervention?
questions.explanation: S. aureus peritonitis with a concurrent exit-site or tunnel infection is unlikely to respond to antibiotics without catheter removal (A); adding rifampin, converting to hemodialysis alone, or fibrinolytic therapy (B/C/D) will not overcome the persistent catheter-associated focus. The infected catheter and tunnel act as a nidus that antibiotics cannot sterilize, so removal is required. This mirrors the general principle that catheter-related infection with tunnel involvement, and refractory or fungal peritonitis, mandate catheter removal.
